critic_raw:
1. OVERSAMPLING DROPOUT INSTANCES IN TRAINING BUT NOT IN VALIDATION/TEST CREATES A DISTRIBUTIONAL MISMATCH THAT INFLATES PERFORMANCE METRICS. The design applies oversampling to the training data to address class imbalance. Oversampling changes the class prevalence in training, which alters the model's predicted probability calibration — the model learns to predict dropout probabilities consistent with the oversampled (artificial) prevalence, not the true prevalence. When the model is evaluated on the validation and test sets (which have the original imbalanced class distribution), the predicted probabilities are miscalibrated. AUPRC, the primary metric, is affected by calibration because it integrates over precision-recall tradeoffs. A miscalibrated model will have a different optimal threshold than one trained on the true distribution. Evidence: compare model performance with and without oversampling on the test set; also report calibration plots to confirm that the oversampled model's predicted probabilities match test-set prevalence.

2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON MULTI-YEAR STUDENT INTERACTION DATA IGNORES TEMPORAL STUDENT COHORT EFFECTS. Student engagement with online courses changes systematically over academic years: curriculum revisions, instructor changes, platform feature rollouts, and pandemic-era enrollment shifts create structural breaks in engagement patterns. A random split will place interactions from the same academic year in both training and test sets. A model trained on this split cannot detect whether it generalizes to new student cohorts or new course versions. The design partially acknowledges this ('exogenous factors such as course revisions are partly mitigated') but does not address temporal cohort effects in the split strategy. Evidence: split by academic year (train on earlier years, test on the most recent year) and compare to random split performance.

3. BERT-BASED MODEL ON STUDENT INTERACTION LOGS IS ARCHITECTURALLY MISMATCHED WITH THE DATA MODALITY. BERT was designed for natural language text with grammar, syntax, and semantic structure. Student interaction logs (course access frequency, forum participation, assignment engagements) are structured behavioral event sequences, not natural language. Applying BERT to 'interaction text data' either means (a) converting behavioral events to text strings (e.g., 'student accessed course 3 times on Monday'), which is an arbitrary serialization that BERT's tokenizer is not designed for, or (b) using actual text from forum posts, which is only a subset of the interaction data. The design does not specify what text BERT processes. If BERT processes serialized behavioral logs, the linguistic pre-training is irrelevant and the model is effectively a large sequence model applied to tabular data with an unnecessary tokenization step. Evidence: clarify what text inputs BERT receives and whether a simpler sequence model (LSTM or gradient boosted tree on aggregated features) performs comparably.

4. THE BASELINE USES ONLY DEMOGRAPHIC FEATURES, MAKING IT AN UNFAIRLY WEAK COMPARATOR. The logistic regression baseline uses 'only demographic attributes' while the NLP model uses both interaction text and engineered metrics. A fair comparison would test whether NLP adds value beyond the best available non-NLP model — which would include all the engineered interaction metrics available to the NLP model, not just demographics. The design tests 'NLP model with everything' versus 'logistic regression with demographics only,' which tests feature set more than model architecture. Evidence: include a logistic regression with the same engineered interaction features (not just demographics) as a second baseline to isolate the NLP contribution.

5. AUPRC AS PRIMARY METRIC IS APPROPRIATE FOR IMBALANCED CLASSIFICATION, BUT EARLY WARNING UTILITY REQUIRES TIME-INDEXED EVALUATION. The stated objective is 'early identification of at-risk students.' For early warning systems, what matters is not just whether the model correctly classifies dropout students but when in the course progression it can identify them. A model that correctly predicts dropout at week 14 of a 15-week course provides little actionable lead time. The design does not evaluate prediction performance as a function of how early in the course the prediction is made. Evidence: compute AUPRC at each week of the course and show that the NLP model's advantage appears early enough for intervention.
all_issues_raised: Oversampling in training miscalibrates predicted probabilities relative to test-set prevalence, affecting AUPRC
Stratified random split ignores temporal student cohort effects from curriculum revisions and platform changes
BERT architecture is architecturally mismatched with structured behavioral event logs rather than natural language
Logistic regression baseline uses demographics only, not all interaction features — creates unfair comparison
AUPRC does not capture early warning utility — time-indexed prediction performance is not evaluated
Oversampling sequence relative to train/test split is unspecified — if applied before splitting, synthetic sample ancestry creates leakage
Random split on timestamped interaction data allows model to train on later-trajectory interactions from dropout students it is being evaluated on earlier in their trajectory
BERT applied to behavioral frequency counts rather than natural language text — mechanism for improvement over simpler models is not established
AUPRC sensitivity to positive rate varies across academic years with different dropout rates — comparisons may reflect base rate differences rather than model quality
Acknowledged uncontrolled course revision confound means model may detect curriculum changes rather than student risk signals
Oversampling order of operations is ambiguous; pre-split SMOTE would cause synthetic sample leakage
BERT encoding conflates student content style with behavioral engagement patterns
Temporal autocorrelation in semester-based dropout is strong; random split mixes early and late-semester engagement contexts
Demographic-only baseline is too weak; behavioral feature logistic regression would isolate the NLP contribution
Asymmetric validation strategy gives logistic regression cross-validated selection stability that BERT model lacks
